summary: Spolsky argues that most software managers understand what good office space looks like but are powerless to achieve it due to long leases and being excluded from space-planning decisions. He highlights the absurdity of software teams being the last consulted about their own work environment. As the founder of his own company, Spolsky announces he has the authority to fix this problem and links to his detailed 'Bionic Office' article describing the solution.
key_points: Software managers understand good office design but lack organizational power to influence it
Office space decisions are made without consulting the people who actually use the space
Company ownership gives Spolsky the autonomy to solve a problem most managers can only complain about
money_quotes: Office space seems to be the one thing that nobody can get right and nobody can do anything about.

The last person anybody asks about how to design the space is the manager of the software team, who finds out what his new veal-fattening pens, uh, cubicle farm is going to be like for the first time on the Monday after the move-in.
Well, it's my own damn company and I can do something about it, so I did.
key_insight: Software teams are systematically excluded from office space decisions that directly affect their productivity, and fixing this requires real organizational authority.
